The letter which Mr. Tyrrell, of the Sonnet, and like-
wise Registrar of the Parochial University, addressed to
the Intermediate Board shows the straits in which the
burst bubble of College Green is in for students. The
net of Trinity is now spread for certain boys and girls
who have distinguished themselves in the Middle and
Senior Grade Intermediate Examinations. Lads and
gitls of from about 16 to 18 are wanted badly by this
school in which Herrings 15 and 16 were branded. A
few sound Dublin Bay herrings that might be branded
in the light of day would be a welcome addition to the
motley gathering of sons of Sourface shopkeepers, par-
sons, farmers, and the like, who inhabit, though they
shed no lustre on Trinity. Mr. Tyrrell, of the Sonnet,
writing in prose to the Intermediate Board, explains the
net that has been made by the highly over-paid Provost
and Senior Fellows, and by Sir John Nutting. Mr. Tyr-
rell says : —* These exhibitions are open to boys and girls
alike.” They are not required to live in the College, or
necessarily to attend lectures, though this latter is
strongly recommended.”  Anything to draw students.
One can almost feel pity for the poor burst bubble of
College Green. Here we have it offering inducements to
students to use it as a mere examining body. No doubt
Trinity offers a good chance for stale fish getting out on
the market ; but now that its medical degrees have been
discredited, and its whole reputation as a teaching insti-
tution damaged, many brilliant boys will naturally pre-
fer to secure their degrees at the R.U.I. The Provost
would look pretty as a “sandwich-man.”’  He might
catch something if he and Tyrrel went up and down Graf-
ton Street with an orange poster for’ad and a green

CURRENT AFRAIRSE,

Conciliating the present Governmeut that propose to
rob Nationalist Ireland of twenty-two seats is evidently
given up by Mr. William Q’Brien as a bad job. “Now
that they had ruined their chances with the Tories,”
said Mr. O’Brien at Watergrasshill, “ we should be glad
to have the Liberals in as quickly as possible.”” ~ Mr.
O’Brien suggested. the holding of a National Conference.
- His-suggestion is that the Chairmen of the County
~ Councils, Lord Mayors and Mayors should call together

a great Council of the nation in Dublin, and that all

manner of Irishmen who are agreed upon the abolition

of landlordism, and who are agreed upon seeking some
eat measure of national relief from misgovernment
‘and over-taxation, should be freely invited and wel-
_ comed. He saw no reason why Mr. Sloan, Lord Dun-
raven, Mr. T. W. Raussell, Mr. Redmond, Mr. Dlllor},

Mr, Healy and their friends, leaders of the Gaelic

League, and every other section and denomination,
should not be represented.

" What would be the scope of such a Conference? If,
as a result, some common denominators could be dis-
‘covered, the Conference might serve a useful purpose.

suggestion, anyway, is a welcome change from

poster aft—Fresh Herrings badly wanted. Large bonus
given for good fish; enough, and to spare, of stale
“saved’ fish in stock. Having got into trouble on ac-
count of some stale fish, we are now buyers of good,
fresh, non-saved fish at any price. Money no object.”’

Father Curry, P.P., of Drogheda, did a good stroke
in the cause of Tolerance, Justice and Fair Play by
writing to the Town Clerk of Drogheda concerning a
“ no-Papist-need-apply '’  advertisement which  the
Boyne Weaving Co., Ltd., of Drogheda, inserted in a
paper called the Ulster Kcho. The advertisement is as
follows :—“Wanted, Lady Typist (Protestant), accus-
tomed to factory work. Apply by letter, giving full
particulars, to Boyne Weaving Company, Ltd.,
Drogheda.” TFather Curry states that a similar adver-
tisement, but without the qualification Protestant, ap-
peared in the Drogheda papers. He asks, “ Was it in-
tended to conceal in Drogheda the shameful policy re-
vealed by the omitted word?’-

The result of Father Curry having written to the
Town Clerk about the matter was that there was a dis-
cussion upon it in the Corporation, and the following
resolution was unanimously passed:—“That we con-
demn in the strongest possible terms the gross piece of

mean and intolerant bigotry brought to our notice by
the timely letter of Father Curry.”

Press exposures are important, but they need to be
supported by action; and the chief way to insure that
they will stand a reasonable chance of being followed
by action is that there should be a special organisation
to look after the temporal interests of Catholics who
suffer commercially because of their creed and breed.
Yet another half-yearly meeting of the Provincial Bank,
that hot bed of Sourfaces, has passed and gone and not
even a solitary protest was raised against the awful dis-
closures set out in this paper of January 30th, 1904,
concerning the staffing of that bank. We showed then
that of the general staff of five, all were  Saved,’” four
likewise being Scotch; on the management staff fifty-
two were “Saved’ and two Papists; amongst the
tellers and accountants seventy-five were ““ Saved ’’ and
cight Papists, and on the clerical staff one hundred and
seventy were ““ Saved ” and twenty-nine Papists; in all,
three hundred and two were “ Saved '’ against a miser-
able twenty-nine *“Idolators.”” And yet there was no
protest ; no demand that the clerkships shonld be thrown
open to competitive examination. Why a bank doing
a highly competitive business is a most sensitive cor-
poration, and even a slight movement on the part of
some shareholders and customers would soon bring the
management to reason. However, we will alter all
that in good time.

Now we have shown up a goodly number of such ad-
vertisements as that in the Ulster Echo, concerning
which Father Curry took such commendable action, and
if local people of influence made a move such as that
happily made by Father Curry, a good volume of work
would have been done for the cause of Tolerance and
Fair Play. We have for some time discontinued looking
over the exclusive-dealing advertisements in the “Zrish’’
T4mes. In view of this Drogheda affair we glanced
through the advertisements in the libeller of Canon
McInerney, and we give the following selection—only a
selection—from that particular day’s paper:—‘“Second
Keeper required for Emo Park, Protestant, married,
small family ; Wife to mind gate lodge ; must be a good
rabbit trapper and vermin killer, and have knowledge
of pheasant rearing. Apply, with full particulars and
copies of discharges, to B. FitzIlerbert, Esq., Emo,
Portarlington. Wanted, by 17th of August, quiet,
Protestant Butler; married man who would go single
preferred; state age, wages expected, and send copies
of papers.—Address ‘Z 3173, Butler,’ this office. Boy
wanted, Protestant, indoor, milk and generally useful;
state wages and where employed.—Langara, Kingstown.
Butler, young, single, Protestant, where Footman kept,
Wanted 8th August; state wages, send papers. Re-
quired for gentleman’s Laundry, near Dublin, an Under

for Co. Waterford, General Farm Labourer, I.C., able
to plough and milk.—Address ‘Z 3305, Man,’ this
office. Clerk—Wanted, young Lady ; Protestant; keep
hooks, and take charge of cash; in city tailoring busi-
ness; must have first-class references; state sillzu"y‘ re-
quired (dinner and tea provided).—Reply, own writing,
“A 1684, this office. Wanted, Costs and General Clerk

Apply, with copies testimonials, and stating Jowest com-

Man,’ this office.”” Now if all these exclusive-dealers
in these various classes of persons who want to earn an
honest livelihood from law-clerks, book-keepers, and

Years ago the public were led to expect the publica-
tion of statistics relating to the trade between Ireland
and Britain. Since the death of Mr. Coyne, who we
understand was looking after it, the matter appears to
have dropped. However, we note that in answer to a
question by Mr. Boland, the Orangemen’s Chief Sec-
retary, announced that the first publication of these
statistics would take place about the end of this year.
We are so long waiting for these statistics that we will
believe they are published when we receive the volume.
The work appears to he so practical and important that
one experiences a mild shock at being told that the
Department (Scotch) is really engaged upon it.

Are we a rebel people? Sometimes we are—on paper
anyway ; and what is more significant, even on paper
sometimes we are not. We read a long account in the
Wezford Peuple of a presentation to one  Master
Tommy Harman’’ of Wexford, where the major part
of the ’98 struggle was fought out. The Wezford
Pecople informs us:—“ A very pleasing and indeed a
noteworthy function was witnessed on Thursday evening
at Carrigbyrne House (the residence of Major and Mrs.
Harman, when the representatives of the recently-pur-
chased tenants came together as independent freemen
to present to the heir of the house, Master Tommy
Harman, a promising youth of nineteen, a beautiful
illuminated address, in recognition of the past many,
but unforgotten, kindnesses of the family, and of the
personal kindness and exceptionally friendly disposition
of the recipient.  7'he occasion of Master Harman join-
ing the army was that which was availed of by the ex-
tenantry to pay this well-deserved tribute.”” The italics
are ours. Our contemporary proceeds to point out that
the presentation was disinterested and spontaneous, for
that the tenants having purchased their land, there was
no room for a display of gratitude for favours to come.
Master Tommy, “a promising youth of nineteen,”” en-
tered the British army, and a sort of spontaneous com-
bustion set up in the hearts of these Wexford tenants,
and an illuminated address like a firework burst out of
the conflagration.

Before we get to the address, let us have a look at
those connected with this Tommy Atkins function in
the County Wexford. The following were amongst
those present on behalf of the Presentation Committec—
Very Rev. Canon O’Brien, P.P., Newbawn; James L.
Doyle, Carrigbyrne; Martin Kenny, Courthoyle;
Joseph Doyle, Raheenakennedy ; Thomas Doyle, Sculla-
bogue ; Edward Nevill, Bawnmore; Allen Wyse Clarke,
M.D., Scullabogue; James Keohoe, Ballyshannon.

The addvess, the Weaford People tells us, contained
“a portrait of Master Harman in the military raiment
he is about to assume.” Canon O’Brien declared that
he had taken part in many proceedings in his lifetime,
bub that he had never experienced greater joy than.on
the present occasion! ~And the occasion, be it
remembered, was that of the presentation of an address
to a youth who was about to become a commissioned
Tommy Atkins! God help poor Ireland. It would
be far better if the youth stopped at home and did
some usoful work in the industrial way. But if he
did that there would be no illuminated address. We
must be very ignorant or else Canon O’Brien must have
been laying the PLam4p on with a shovel. “The fame

We are a curious people.

At the recent County Antrim Assizes the 23 men
sworn on the Grand Jury were all “ Saved,”’” and they
included such well-known true blues as Anthony Traill,
bigot-Provost of the poor old Parochial University of
Herrings 15 and 16 fame, that is now touting for In-
termediate boys to bolster up its fottering fortunes; and
William Moore, K.C., M.P., J.P. At the recent County
Down Assizes the twenty-two men sworn on the Grand
Jury were also all “Saved.””

The Aurora Bazaar at Bray has been a great success.
It was organised to help to pay off the debt on the
“ Idolators’ > churches in Bray parish. The event was
the occasion of a display of Sourface bigotry.  The
real anti-Irish Sourface would not allow an “ Idola-
tors’ >’ Church to stand in the country if he could help
it; if he had power he would raze all the Catholic
churches to the ground, and leave nothing in that ws
standing but the various classes of dissenting chapels.
It is very embittering to the real Sourface that the
Church has prevailed against him, and he indiscreetly
boils over in public sometimes.

‘We print a copy of the circular that was distributed
last week. It is a significant document, and owr rea-
ders would do well to study it. Emboldened by many
circumstances, including the University debacle and the
drumming campaign that resulted in the Orangemen
: getting Walter Long as Chief Secretary, there has been
B quite a Sourface Revival of recent months in this coun-
try. The circular ran as follows:—

7 “Tt is sincerely hoped that the Protestants of Bray,
and, indeed, of Ircland, will have sufficient seli-reslzect
to prevent their aiding the local habitat of Romanism
by attending the ‘ Aurora’ Bazaar (a roaring Bizarre)

I during the present week. o

“This is all the more apparent when it is pointed
out that this Bazaar is—

“(1) A Sabbath-breaking Bazaar. :

“(2) A gambling, rafiling, game of chance Bazaar.
“(3) A Bazaar in aid of funds of the Church
whose - members never support any Protestant
charity.

hence all Protestant parents are unmarried, and,

«(5) It is in aid of a Church which teaches far
more the worship of a woman—the Virgin Mary
—than it does that of our Divine Lord Jesus

rity of Ireland, and is only restrained by the

0 “Don’t help it.

“Be a Protestant.

This outrageous production calls for no comment from

us. It is sufficient to lay it as it stands before our

readers.

The speeches of ““ The Twelfth,”’ the gloating of the
Irish Times over the proposed robbing of Ireland of
twenty-two ~Parliamentary seats, and this outrageous
circular all spring from the one bitter anti-Irish and
anti-Catholic impulse. ~ There has been, for various
reasons, a revival of this anti-Irish spirit; and if the
“Idolators ” do not organise to meet it, they will de-
serve little or no pity if they are gradually cut up in
detail.

Both Father Kavanagh and Mr.
the occasion of the unveiling o
Murphy Memorial at Tullow on Sunday, in
scathing language to the Irishmen who take service in
the British army. Father Kavanagh said:—“If the
majority of professed Irishmen were sincere in their
profession, would England find in the Irish people a
nation from which she always could recruit her army
and navy, a people so many of whom preferred her
manufactures to their own, and every year poured mil-
lions into her treasury by indulgence in intoxicating
drinks?’ Mr. Davitt said :—“ Why should any Irish-
man join the English army? (hear, hear). Can any
honest, self-respecting countryman offer a solitary rea-
son why the same army is, or ought to be, one iota less
objectionable to young Irishmen than the Russian army
is to the men of Finland?’ (applause). It is well to
keep those questions before the public mind even though
it be plain that men enlist largely through force of
cconomic pressure, or for lack of character to face the
material problem of existence in civil walks of life.
The Anti-Emigration League has done much good even
though it alone cannot stop emigration; and references
such as we have quoted from Father Kavanagh and
Mr. Davitt serve a wholesome purpose, even though the
problem of enlisting in the English army is a wide and
complex one; it is largely due to Anglicisation—a huge
problem in itself—and largely to economic conditions,
that it is very difficult, indeed, to get the nation to pay
serious and adequate heed to.

Mr. Davitt, by the way, made some severe remarks
concerning the patriotism of Dublin, which city he said
ought to feel ashamed of itself for not having yet
erected a monument to the memory of the men of ’98.
“I am not aware,’”’ said he, “ that Dublin contributes
anything worth talking about just now to any national
movement or purpose. It offers no monetary support to
the Irish Party or to the United Irish League, and can-
not, therefore, plead the generosity of its aid in one
direction, as an excuse for leaving the foundation stone
of the Wolfe Tone monument as bare of superstructure
to-day as it was seven years ago.’’

The Rev. William MisKimmin, now of Falkirk, and 
one time of Newry, was one of the heroes who led the 
poor deluded Orange hinds to Rostrevor. 
He was a 
highly susceptible man, and fell an easy victim to love 
and terror. 
He was terrified of the Idolators ; he is 
now to pay, if he has not already paid, £100 to a young 
lady to whose charms he fell a victim, for this impetuous 
lcacle,· of the poor Orange dupes evidently declared hi~ in-
tentions before he knew his own mind. It appears from 
the evidence at the breach of promise case that he often 
spoke of his fear of attacks from the Catholics. 
The 
fickle Orange leader, meandering through the pictu-
resque lanes with the lady of his heart, spoke to her of 
giving up the ministry, becoming a lawyer, entering 
Parliament, and attacking the Nationalist Party. He 
told one Mr. Smith that he had been offered £1,000 a 
year in _Falkirk. 
As he is now Minister of the Gospel 
Temperance 0hurch at Falkirk, we assume that that is 
the post to fill which he was offered £1,00Q. a year. 
It 
shocks our "economic sense " that such_ a huge salary 
should be lavished 0 11 such a post as that. 
'I'hough 
Rev. Mr. MisKimmin chafed under the restraints of 
obscm·e life in Newry, and thirsted for fame on the 
floor of the British House of Commons, and for on-
slaughts on the Irish Nationalist Party there, though 
he dreaded the " Idolators,' ' yet this :fickle gentleman, 
according to evidence, addressed the lady to whom he 
lrn.s now to pay £100 as " acushla machree." 
One 
night whilst. sitting in a parlour the susceptible Rev. 
MisKimmin told the lady that he had " fallen a vic-
tim ; " he added that he was a commander of thousands 
of men, but that he had never found a woman who 
swayed him as she did. 
Oh, fickle Mr. MisKimmin, 
we weep for you ! What do the thousands of men under 
your command think of you now 1 
His fear of the 
" Iclolators " was so intense that he always carried a 
revolver. 
In Liverpool this doughty commander of 
thousands of men proposed marriage to her who sw:ayecl 
him as no other woman did. In the court he denied 
the promise! 
According to ev.idence, on the occasion on 
which he made it, he told the object of his affections 
that there was another, but that he would never ma.ny 
that other, for the false one, unlike George Washing-
ton, had spoken an untruth. 
The compact was made, 
and Mr. MisKimmin, leader of thousands of. men, liv-
ing under the shadow of a great fear of the children of 
"superstition," asked his lady-love what kind of en-
gagement ring she would like. 
The lady said she w~uld 
like an emerald ring, but this rev. cnild of light and 
enemy of superstition and idolatry remarked that a.n 
emerald was ··1ml·ttd,:11, and so presented her with a dia-
mond ring. 
And in due course this leader of thousands 
of men married-another woman. 
Oh, fie, :fie, Mr. 
MisKimmin. 
Not being superstitious ourselves, we 
weep for your obscurant dread of emerald; but it is 
evident that tlrn,t clia.moncl ring was, at least, most un-
fortunate.

THE BOARD OF WWORKS.

GREAT many of the rank and file of the staff of

the Board of Works are civil servants who have
secured their position in open competition. Naturally,
therefore, there are a good many Catholics in the rank
and file posts. But if an examination gets you in it is,
in  great measure, patronage that gets you
on in the higher walks where, we pre-
sume, the Masonic sign and the Orange
influence are not unknown. We are not surprised to
find that the great majority of the superior posts are
held by the “ Saved.”” The Board itself is mainly Eng-
lish in its constitution. The Chairman of the Board
and the two Commissioners, drawing together £3,500
a year in salaries, are all “Saved.” In the Secretary’s
Department the Secretary and Assistant Secretary are
“Baved.” A large number of the Second Division mez
are Papists, no thanks to anyone but their own ability
to pass the examination. In the Secretary’s Depart-
ment 11 “Saved ”’ draw £3,094 or an average of £281,
whilst 16 ““ Idolators’’ draw £2,957, or an average of
about, £185. N

In the Land Improvement and Land Act Loans De-
partment, the Examiner, Assistant Examiner, twelve
Inspectors and the Drainage Clerk are “Saved,” four
Inspectors are “Idolators.’”” In this Department the
“Saved ”’ draw £4,858 a year, and the “Idolators’’
only draw £1,400. R

In the Accountants’ Branch, the Accountant and
Assistant Accountant are “ Saved.”” Five of the Second
Division Clerks are Saved’ whilst thirteen of the
Second Division Clerks—the Second Division Clerks
must pass a competitive examination— are “ Idolators.”’
It is in the average that the economic advantage of
being “Saved’’ shows itself in this Department.
Eleven “Saved’ officials of this Department draw
£3,617 or an average of about £329, whilst exactly
double the number of ““Idolators,”” largely made up of
Second Division Clerks, who won their right to em-
ployment at open examination, draw £3,568, or an
average per “ Idolator ”’ of about £162.

In the Engineers’ Department three “ Saved ’ officials
draw £1,450 between them, whilst a solitary  Idolator’
gets £200 a year.

The Architectural Department is the worst of all.
The three principal Surveyors are all “Saved.”” Out
of a total staff of 26 no less than twenty-two are

“Saved ”’; the “Saved ’’ draw £6,870 and the “ Idola- .

tors ”’ less than one-sixth that sum, or, to be precise,
£1,065 per annum,

The total number of “ Saved ’’ on the Board and in
the Secretary’s, Land Improvement and Land Act
Loans, Accountants, Engineers, Turniture, and Archi-
tectural Department is sixty-six, and they draw be-
tween them £23,615; the total number of “ Idolators—
largely Second Division men—in the same Department is
47, and they draw £9,490. The average for the sixty-
six “Saved’ is about £358 per annum; the average
for the forty-seven “ Idolators’’ is about £202.

It is the old story, only somewhat less so in conse-
quence of the large number of officials who forced their
way in through the open competition door.  What
chance have “Idolators’ of any promotion that goes

by patronage in this Castle Department? Of course

T]us., _dcar reader, is I think a statement you are not un-

Irish, nor yet to enquire how far French 4s a useful
language, and what proportion of the many who are
taught that subject in Irish schools succeed in earning
even as much as thirty-five shillings by it in a life-time.
After listening to Dr. O’Daly’s and Mr. O’Keane’s lec-
tures at Ballingeary, I think that still another reply is
possible, and  that is that if you want
to learn French or German, you . had better
begin by learning Irish, for I  venture
to think that were a man really compelled of a sudden
to learn Continental languages for some purpose, the
‘man who had mastered Trish would probably know more
of them in six months than the boy who had been
drizzling French all his school days.

I do not say, however, that this result will necessarily
follow from every attendance at an Irish class. Alas!
how many enthusiastic travellers along the path of Irish
studies lie sleeping round the mile-stone of a third or
fourth O’Growney, while even those of us who have
passed the fifth stadium, sit marooned upon some tuft of
heath in the midst of the far-extending bog. A visit to
Ballingeary, even if it be not long enough to teach you
Irish, will, at any rate, explain to you why you have not
succeeded in learning it—a sad, reproachful query which
has occwrred to many of us. The answer is a truly
simple one; Irish is not taught in Ireland simply be-
cause no language is taught there.

Some time ago, I wrote an article on the question of
“ Standards,”” in which I pointed out that one great
obstacle in Ireland was that we took the English maxi-
mum as our standard, and desired merely that our rail-
ways should be as good as English railways, our workmen
as efficient as English workmen, our University up to the
standard of Oxford and Cambridge; while all the
time we forgot the fact that it was in Germany and
America that these things were done best, and that there
were to be found the ideals towards which we should
seek to strive. In the matter of education this finds a
very practical illustration. The “ happy English child *’
is, without doubt, the worst taught of any that are
brought up in a civilised country, and yet for practical
purposes he is taken as the standard of education in Ire-
land, whether by means of Mr. Dale or of a Royal Com-
mission. Now bad as all departments of English teach-
ing are, the teaching of modern languages is an abomin-
ahle worst, and the child aforesaid is equally happy in
his inability to pronounce French and to talk German.
Yet it is the English method of instruction that is uni-
versally employed in regard to these subjects in. Ireland,
and not unnaturally the same system is applied when it
comes to teaching us. The absence of any scientific
method of language-teaching has been the real difficulty
with which so many thousands of learners have had to

contend. - g 3 5

and not words, is his principle for elementary teaching.
How many, for instance, of the several thousand pupils
who were, last year, taught to mispronounce the French
word pére in Irish schools really knew that in its true
Parisian pronunciation the last consonant sound is a
“g,”’ which is practically the same as the Irish
aspirated “ g%’

Mr. O’Keane devotes himself to a different phase of
the subject. He is a master hand at the Berlitz or ob-
ject-lesson method of instruction, which has, of course,
already been adopted by Mr. Kent and some other Irish
teachers. By this system the knowledge glides into
one’s mind as it were unnoticed, and you are talking the
language hefore you have well begun it. If the Irish
teachers whom he is instructing are not expert in that
art when they return to their classes, it will not be his
fault. Should the Berlitz and phonetics method come gen-
erally into use, there will be a fair chance of turning out
Trish speakers instead of the unlimited number of well-
intentioned learners, the “chronic’’ Gaelic Leaguers—
to borrow a medical term—produced under our present
system. A strong feeling is growing that some better
way of teaching Irish is absolutely necessary if the Gaelic
Leaguers are to be able to meet without laughing.  If
the whole thing is not to be humbug, we need something
that shall be at once less irksome in its details and more
practical in its results than our present way of working,
and without necessarily believing in the existence of a
royal road to Irish, the system carried out at Ballin-
geary is the best I have hitherto had any experience of.
Indeed, if the Gaelic League could secure its general
adoption, it would soon compel teachers of other sub-
jects to improve their methods out of very shame.

Ahout Ballingeary itself. Tt is the ideal situation for
such a College, surrounded by an amphitheatre of soft-
shaded, beautiful hills, on the shore of a lovely lake,
with exquisite views, like the back-ground of one of
Raphael’s pictures, ever peeping up brightly in the dis-
tance. It is all a soft, clear Irish harmony by the lovely
waters of the Leap. The study of Irish in the native home
is often associated with anything rather than the joie de
vivre from a physical and digestive point of view, but
here there is excellent accommodation, the houses are
clean and neat, and the people sympathetic. Or if you
seek more, there is the hotel at Inchigeela, within an
casy cycle ride. Such an hotel, too, beautifully sweet
and clean, with a pure draught of fresh air, extremely
cheap,* and swimming in fresh cream and newly-plucked
fruit, everything to delight one who is capable of either
external or internal msthetic pleasure.

The secretary of the College is Mr. O’Scannell, who is
extremely obliging. His boast it might be to say “ Bal-
lingeary, c’est moi,’’ or rather “ip mipe’’ for it is Mr.
O’Scannell’s pupils that have gained for it its proud
title. He presides over the dancing and singing in the
evening, which are very enjoyable; but let it be said, it
1s not all dancing and singing, as in places I wot of ; the
students do a really hard day’s work, and seem
thoroughly enthusiastic in pursuit of their studies, and
how varied they are, from Donegal and from Dublin,
priests, secular and regular, laymen and religious, all
assembled in this out-of-the-way part of the country
with the one object of seeing how Irish may be imparted
by the most scientific methods. Let us trust that when
they again go out amongst the people, they will be foun-
tain-heads both of Irish and of true teaching.

Here I observe in passing that the intention of Eras-

THE ARMY AS A REFORMATORY!

READ with particular interest in the LEADER Sum-
mer Number “Imaal’s” witty account of his
Parliamentary candidature, and of the heckling he was
subject to. Though unable to be present on that occa-
sion, and though debarred by an accident of birth from
being a free and independent elector of there or any-
where else, I hope you will permit me to put in a word,
exactly as I would have had I been present, and entitled
to take part in the pleasant process of heckling. Asked
whether he approved of Irishmen enlisting in the British
army, I was glad to see the candidate only approved it
as an alternative to the army of drunkards and loafers.
There was in this an implied acknowledgment that the
army (though, perhaps, otherwise objectionable) was yet
useful in some undefined way, either as reformatory for
drunkards and loafers, or as a suitable medium for rid-
ding Ireland of them. Now, as a Gaelic League official
who should advisedly be silent on matters political, I say
nothing for or against the army from a political point of
view. “Imaal,”” however, has opened up a point of
social and temperance reform on which even Gaelic
League non-political officials may venture to express an
opinion.  The Industrial and Temperance Movement,
emigration and employment for young men in Ireland,
are permissible subjects of debate on a Gaelic League
platform. Enlisting in the army is a form of emigration
—a more objectionable form—than a mere crossing
of Atlantic. =~ The servant maids and workers in the
States, as a rule, can send something home to help. The
soldier son is taken quite away, has no margin of earn-
ings to send home, not enough even to marry on, nor has

he liberty to marry except under strict regulations.

If he has been a steady boy, lured by the glitter of a
uniform and the sound of the drum, he is a loss to his
family, a loss to his country which has need of workers,
and generally a complete loss to the girl he leaves behind
him. His steadiness and temperance are unlikely to
overcome temptation, if (to quote the laureate of the
English army) they ship him.

“ Somewhere east of Suez, . . . . .
‘Where there ain’t no Ten Commandments.’’

If he survives to come home as a pensioner,
he more than likely has his working - capacity
diminished by a touch of some foreign variety of malaria,
or he may have taken to drink.

member of society, will be (leaving aside political preju-
dice against him) an exceptionally steady and worthy
man. He must be to come through it all, and, on the
whole, there is no working or farming family in this
country but has a right to object to sce a son of the
steady type enlist. His doing it is their loss, not to be
grumbled at if it was their duty to make the sacrifice
from patriotic motives. = Whether it is their duty to
make that sacrifice, I need not argue. Others are free
to do it.

But to come to the point of the utility of the army as
a reformatory. If a drunkard and loafer enlists there
may be a sense of relief. He is got rid of, provided for,
and may be disciplined into a better shape. But he is
only out of the way for a short term of years, comes back
to his unfortunate family, unsuited for country life or
honest work, and unrestrained by the strong hand of
martial discipline, his brutality has no bound. If he has
been held back from over-drinking, he will do it now.
I have not only personal experience, but statistics, to
prove my assertion. Vagrant soldiers and militiamen off
training largely make up the workhouse and tramp popu-
lation of Ireland.

I would commend to your readers who have this sub-
Jject under consideration, a delightful English novel in
which the questions of agriculture and militarism are
prominent. I refer to that master-piece of Thomas
Hardy— Far from the Madding Crowd,”’ in which Ser-
geant Troy may serve as the most typical picture of a
soldier in English literature. The patience, intelligence,
and hard-working qualities of a young farmer are held
up in contrast to the idle glitter of the military man.
This is done not in a second-rate, moralising style, but by
the hand of a great and acknowledged artist—one whose
genius has been extolled and illumined by an Irish
patriot writer, the late Lionel Johnson. I need say no
more on the subject, but will be glad to hear “ ITmaal’s
response as to the suitability of the army as a reforma-
tory; and I hope, moreover, he has read, or will read,
“ Far from the Madding Crowd.”

Under the above title a society, registered under the
Friendly Societies’ Act, has just been started. It aims
at providing its members with a means of investing pro-
fitably small savings; the fund resulting being utilised
among other ways in affording accommodation on easy
terms to small trades people and others to enable them
to strengthen and develop their position. The forma-
tion of this society is another evidence of the spirit of
self-help which has recently sprung up in our people.
Hitherto a trader or other feeling the need of a small
loan to help him over a difficulty, and finding that our
banks did not encourage such business, was often
forced into greater difficulties by subjecting himself to
the tender mercies of the foreign money-lender. Such
a society as the Sinn Fein which, besides affording
necessary accommodation to its members, prcsemfs
the best features of a savings bank, s
to be welcomed, and we heartily commend
its objects, and anticipate for it a prosperous
career. The registered offices are at 78 Rathmines
Road, where all information can be 9bta§ncd every
Monday evening from half-past seven till nine o’clock.

THE CONNACHT IRISH COLLEGE.

7T HE first session of the Irish College of the West is

drawing to a close, and the second session of six
weeks will begin on the first of August. The students
at first session coming from Galway, Mayo, Roscommon,
and even from Derry, Tyrone, and Dublin—there is
one all the way from Liverpool—reached the large num-
ber of about thirty towards the middle of the session.
They have all found lodging in the houses that are
dotted around the College within a radius of a mile.
And it will not be without some importance to know
that these Irish-speaking homes are clean and comfort-
able—almost remarkably so—and that the students find
not only excellent fare in the homely things that are
the pride of the Dean o’ Tize but are also sure of a
good clean bed in a good clean room, whenever they
ave tired of the nightly Seanacus, or stories, or 1eng‘ohy
danta about the might of Fionn and Oisin, or of the
crooning of the pean bean nat seated on the vac. As
several of the students were practically only beginners,
it was found necessary to arrange for them a spcga!
class, and, in addition, to secure for them the services
of a highly qualified assistant teacher, Miss O’Toole.
These pupils were taught chiefly on the lines of the Moo
Réro  system, of which the principal, Mr. Walsh, is
an admirable exponent. They had also to go through
the usual exercises in grammar and composition, and
translation from an easy Irish text. The more ad-
vanced students were able to make a close and critical
study of several of the more difficult tests in prose and
poetry. The first session, from ten to one, was occupied
with text work, composition, storytelling, Seanacus, and
a song or two in the traditional method. The second
session, from three to six, was spent in a somewhat
similar manner, but, at the end of each session, a
special demonstration was given of the Mov KéO
method.

This was intended not ouly for the benefit of the be-
ginners, but also for intending teachers, who, in turn,
were given an opportunity of conducting a lesson in
that method, always, of course, under the supervision
of the principal. Needless to say, every portion of the
day’s work was conducted exclusively in Irish.

It is beyond all doubt that the College, even in the
first session since its foundation, has accomplished a
great deal of valuable work. The coming session
promises to be still more successful and still more largely
attended. It goes without saying that intending stu-
dents should make an effort to be present at the classes
from the very beginning of the session. All information
about the College and lodging arrangements can be
had from the Very Rev. James Corbett, J.P., Partry,
Ballinrobe, who is the local manager of the College
